coredata.dc:title: Photo-physicochemical properties and in vitro photodynamic therapy activity of morpholine-substituted Zinc(II)-Phthalocyanines π-π stacked on biotinylated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Nene, Lindokuhle C.
Managa, Muthumuni
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Two morpholine-substituted Zn(II) phthalocyanines, complex 4 and the cationic 5, were synthesized and conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and biotinylated GQDs (GQDs-biotin) by non-covalent π-π interactions. The GQDs-biotin are prepared as potential nanoparticle-based Pc delivery vector combined with a receptor-mediated transport system using biotin. The photo-physicochemical properties of the Pc complexes and their corresponding conjugates were studied. Upon conjugation, the fluorescence quantum yields decrease for 4 and 5, however, the triplet quantum yields were increased for the conjugates. All samples demonstrated singlet oxygen generation. For conjugated complexes, the singlet quantum yields decreased due to the screening effect in some cases. An increase in the photodynamic therapy activities upon quaternization was observed for the conjugates, with the cell viability decreasing from 66.2% to 51.2% after treatment for 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs, respectively. A relatively better performance was also observed for the cationic complex in combination with the biotin functionalized GQDs, 5-GQDs-biotin, where the cell viability was 34.9% after treatment. Moreover, the cellular uptake of 5-GQDs-biotin over 24 h was relatively higher compared to Pc complex alone and other Pcs-GQDs conjugates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phthalocyanine
Graphene quantum dots
Photodynamic therapy
Morpholine
Biotin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the cationic phthalocyanine 5, Scheme 1
Conjugation of Pcs to GQDs/GQDs-biotin, Scheme 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra showing complex 3 and 4, biotin, pristine GQDs, GQDs-biotin and the 4-GQDs-biotin conjugates.
The EDX spectra showing the chemical composition of A) GQDs and B) GQDs-biotin.
The TEM images of A) GQDs in ethanol, B) GQDs-biotin in methanol and C) 5-GQDs-biotin in ethanol with their respective DLS graphs showing the average dimeter sizes in water.
The Raman spectra of GQDs, GQDs-biotin and 4-GQDs-biotin.
The UV-vis absorption spectra of Pcs 4 and 5 in DMSO and 5 in water.
The UV-vis absorption spectra of A) 4, 4-GQDs and 4-GQDs-biotin and B) 5, 5-GQDs and 5-GQDs-biotin, and C) GQDs and GQDs-biotin in DMSO.
The UV-vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of 4 and 5 in DMSO. Excitation wavelength = 610 nm.

The UV-vis spectra of A) Absorption of 4, emission of GQDs and emission of 4-GQDs and, insert: emission spectrum of 4. B) Absorption of 5, emission of GQDs and emission of 5-GQDs in DMSO, insert: emission spectrum of 5.Excitation wavelength = 355 nm for all samples.
The TCSPC graph showing fluorescence decay of 4 in DMSO, where λ exc 684 nm.
The triplet decay curve of for complex 4 in DMSO.

The singlet oxygen generation by 5 A) in DMSO monitored by the degradation of DPBF over 100 s at 20 s time intervals and B) in water monitored by the degradation of ADMA over 50 min at 10 min time intervals.
The cytograms of MCF7-cells when A) incubated in the dark, B) after PDT in the presence of 5-GQDs-biotin.

Histograms showing the activity of 4-GQDs, 5-GQDs and 5-GQDs-biotin at different concentrations on MCF-7 cells when A) dark incubation and B) irradiation for 5 min N = 3. Control = 1% DMSO in cultured media.
MCF-7 cellular uptake over 24 h as a function of fluorescence intensity, for A) 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs and B) 5-GQDs and 5-GQDs-biotin where λ exc 680 nm, and C) GQDs and GQDs-biotin at λ exc 360 nm.
Reaction scheme for the preparation of complexes 3, 4 and 5.
Reaction scheme for the preparation of GQDs-biotin and conjugation of 4 onto GQDs-biotin. r.t. = room temperature.
Summary of absorbance excitation emission parameters in DMSO and structural characteristics of complexes 4 and 5 and their respective conjugates.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Value in brackets are for samples in water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Excitation wavelength = 610 nm for Pcs and 355 nm for GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the photo-physicochemical properties of 4 and 5 and their respective GQDs and GQDs-biotin conjugates in DMSO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for exciting where GQDs absorb.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of PDT activity at 100 μg/mL of Pcs and Pcs-GQDs on MCF-7 in vitro, n = 3.
Cellular uptake n = 3.

head.ce:title: Photo-physicochemical properties and in vitro photodynamic therapy activity of morpholine-substituted Zinc(II)-Phthalocyanines π-π stacked on biotinylated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two morpholine-substituted Zn(II) phthalocyanines, complex 4 and the cationic 5, were synthesized and conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and biotinylated GQDs (GQDs-biotin) by non-covalent π-π interactions. The GQDs-biotin are prepared as potential nanoparticle-based Pc delivery vector combined with a receptor-mediated transport system using biotin. The photo-physicochemical properties of the Pc complexes and their corresponding conjugates were studied. Upon conjugation, the fluorescence quantum yields decrease for 4 and 5, however, the triplet quantum yields were increased for the conjugates. All samples demonstrated singlet oxygen generation. For conjugated complexes, the singlet quantum yields decreased due to the screening effect in some cases. An increase in the photodynamic therapy activities upon quaternization was observed for the conjugates, with the cell viability decreasing from 66.2% to 51.2% after treatment for 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs, respectively. A relatively better performance was also observed for the cationic complex in combination with the biotin functionalized GQDs, 5-GQDs-biotin, where the cell viability was 34.9% after treatment. Moreover, the cellular uptake of 5-GQDs-biotin over 24 h was relatively higher compared to Pc complex alone and other Pcs-GQDs conjugates.

ce:section.ce:para: The phthalocyanines (Pcs) can be functionalized to allow for a variety of applications including non-linear optics [1], photovoltaics and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [2,3]. Their ability to generate singlet oxygen is of particular interest in PDT. For applications in PDT, Pcs should ideally absorb in the near infrared region (NIR), be effortlessly water soluble, have high singlet oxygen generation ability and have no dark-toxicity to cells [4–6]. The ability of the activated Pcs to interact with ground state molecular oxygen (O2) and produce singlet oxygen is key to initiate a cascade of damaging effects to the cells through oxidative stress [5]. Hence, numerous modifications on Pcs have been done in an attempt to improve their photophysical and photochemical behaviour, including their singlet oxygen quantum yields [7–9].

Several Pcs substituted with morpholine moieties have been reported [10–13]. Dlugaszewska et al. [13] reported on the efficiency of a MPc containing morpholine substituents as a photosensitizer for photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy, which is based on the same principles as PDT. Moreover, morpholine groups are targeted as cancer selective entities in cancer drug development [14]. In this study, a 2,5-dimethyl-4-(morpholinomethyl)phenoxy substituent was employed, which is a bulkier substituent than reported in the literature [10–13]. The bulky substituents will enhance solubility and reduce aggregation in organic solvents, and therefore may improve singlet oxygen generating ability [15]. Additionally, the nitrogen on the morpholine can be quaternized, resulting in water solubility of the Pc complexes [16]. Furthermore, cationic Pcs are not only beneficial for solubility purposes, but have also been reported to be attracted to the anionic membrane of cancer cells and tend to accumulate at the tumor site based on opposite charge attraction [17], consequently improving selectivity and therapeutic efficacy.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based, aromatic, luminescent and photoactive nano-structures [18,19], which have been conjugated to Pcs both covalently or non-covalently (through π-π stacking) [18]. The GQDs are reported to be important for targeting and delivery of therapeutic agents to the tumor site [19], they are also known to have low toxicity compared to semiconductor quantum dots [19]. Thus, GQDs are currently being studied for a wide spectrum of bio/medicinal applications including drug delivery. GQDs can further be decorated with tumor targeting biomolecules such as folic acid and biotin, in this study, we covalently link biotin to the GQDs.

Cancer cells differ from non-cancer cells by over-expressing certain proteins in order to maintain their rapid proliferation and metastasis. In cancer cells, the biotin receptor protein is overexpressed [20]. This serves as an advantage for cancer drug delivery design, where cancer therapeutics are tagged/labelled with biotin to target cancer cells [21] and increase the uptake using the biotin receptor-mediated transport system. For the formation of covalent bonds, the carboxylic-terminal of biotin is usually used, since the specific binding group of the biotin is the ring, which fits into the binding cavity of biotin receptor [22]. In this work, we use amino-functionalized GQDs to provide amine groups that will form amide bonds with the carboxyl-end of the biotin.

The aim of this work is to prepare neutral and cationic morpholine-substituted Pcs. The Pcs will then be conjugated to biotin-functionalized graphene quantum dots, and thereafter we study the PDT performance of the Pcs when conjugated to the GQDs.

All the solvents and reagents used in this study were of reagent grade quality and were purchased from commercial suppliers. The 2,5-dimethyl-4-(morpholino methyl) phenol hydrochloride monohydrate (2), 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0] undec-7-ene (DBU), zinc acetate (Zn(OAc)2; 99%), dimethyl sulfate (DMS), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 4-dimethylamino)pyridine (DMAP), biotin, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), anthracene-9,10-bis-methylmalonate (ADMA), quinine sulfate, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF) and the unsubstituted Zn(II) phthalocyanine (ZnPc) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich®. Millipore water was acquired from a Milli-Q water system (Millipore Corp, Bedford, MA, USA). Human breast adenocarcinoma Michigan cancer −7 cell line (MCF-7) were purchased from Cellonex®. The Dulbecco phosphate-buffer saline, Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10% (v/v)) and the 100 μg/mL-penicillin:100 unit/mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B-mixture (PSA) as well as the neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST) were all purchased from Biowest®. The 4-nitrophthalonitrile (1), Scheme 1
, was synthesized as described in the literature [23]. The GQDs containing carboxylic, hydroxyl and amino functional groups were synthesized as reported [24].
The equipment items employed in this work are listed in the Supplementary Information.

To obtain the 4-(2,5-dimethyl-4-(morpholinomethyl)phenoxy)phthalonitrile (3), 2,5-dimethyl-4-(morpholinomethyl)-phenol hydrochloride monohydrate (2) was first suspended in Millipore H2O and filtered to remove the HCl dissolved in the H2O. The solid was thereafter dissolved in CHCl3 and anhydrous Na2SO4 was added to the solution to remove residual H2O. The solid was collected by filtration and dried under pressure in vacuo. The dry light-brown solid (2) (3 g, 14.5 mmol) and 4-nitrophthalonitrile (1) (2 g, 11.55 mmol) were dissolved in dry DMF (15 mL). Potassium carbonate (5 g, 36.18 mmol) was added into the reaction mixture in 1 g portions after 1 h intervals. The reaction mixture was maintained at ambient temperature in N2 gas with continuous stirring for 48 h. The product was precipitated with ice and collected through vacuum filtration, dried and recrystalized in acetone. Yield: 68%, Anal. Calc. for C21H21N3O2, expected: C, 72.60; H, 6.09; N 12.10%, found: C, 71.41; H, 6.30; N, 12.00%. IR [(ATR), Vmax/cm−1]: 2961 (Ar, CH), 2853 (Cto H), 2233 (-CN), 1674 (Ar, C=C), 1600 (Ar, C=N), 1093 (COC). NMR: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3), (δ:ppm): 8.05 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 1H, ArH), 7.87 (s, 1H, ArH), 7.23 (m, 2H, ArH), 6.91 (s, 1H, ArH), 3.78–3.76 (m, 4H, CH2 H), 3.61 (s, 2H, CH2 H), 2,71 (s, 4H, CH2 H), 2.29 (s, 3H, CH3 H), 2.04 (s, 3H, CH3 H).

The zinc ((2,9,16,23-tetrakis-(2,5-dimethyl-4-(morpholinomethyl))-phenoxy) phthalocyanine (4) was obtained through cyclo-condensation of 3 (0.5 g, 1.5 mmol) dissolved in 1–pentanol (3 mL) in the presence of zinc acetate (Zn(OAc)2) (0.4 g, 2 mmol) and DBU (0.3 mL). The mixture was heated at 140 °C under N2 atmosphere and left to stir for 24 h. The reaction mixture was thereafter cooled to ambient temperature. The product was precipitated out of solution with 1:1 methanol and water solution (v/v) and centrifuged at 3500 rpm, then finally washed with methanol and then collected by centrifugation. The pelleted product was then air-dried in an enclosed fume hood. The product was further purified using silica gel column, eluted with 1:1 (v/v) chloroform and tetrahydrofuran (THF) as the eluting solvent mixture. Yield: 82.5%, Anal. Calc. for C84H84N12O8Zn, Expected C, 69.34; H, 5.82; N, 11.55%. Found: C,69.80; H, 5.70; N, 11.21%. MALDI-TOF-MS (m/z): calculated [M] 1455.02 m/z. Found 1455.02 m/z. IR [(ATR), Vmax/cm−1]: 3000 (Ar, CH stretch), 2817 (CH), 1720 (Ar, C=N), 1211 (CN), 1000 (COC). NMR: 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3), (δ:ppm): 8.07 (d, J = 9.2 Hz, 2H, ArH), 7.75 (m, 4H, ArH), 7.0–7.25 (m, 6H, ArH), 6.6–6.71 (m, 8H, ArH), 3.75–4.0 (s, 11H, CH2 H), 3.70 (s, 3H, CH2 H), 3.3 (m, 10H, CH2 H), 2.55–2.25 (m, 16H, CH2 H), 2.18 (s, 4H, CH3 H), 1.75 (d, 20H, CH3 H). UV-vis (DMSO): λ max 684 nm, UV/Vis λ max (nm) (log ɛ): 684 (4.87), 615 (4.24), 355 (4.6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the cationic phthalocyanine 5, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain complex 5, a solution of 4 (80 mg, 0.055 mmol) in anhydrous DMF was prepared and stirred at 90 °C for 30 min under Ar gas. Excess DMS was thereafter added drop-wise and the mixture stirred for 48 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature, solid product was washed with hot acetone, and centrifuged, and thereafter continuously washed with ethanol, ethyl acetate, THF and chloroform, dried with diethyl ether and left to dry further in fume hood. Yield: 67.5%, UV-vis (DMSO): λ max 682 nm. Anal. Calc. for C88H96N12O8Zn, expected: C, 69.76; H, 6.39; N, 11.09; Found: C, 67.9; H, 6.82; N, 11.2%. IR [(ATR), Vmax/cm−1]: 2996 (Ar, CH), 2807 (CH), 1611 (Ar, C=N), 1241 (CN), 1000 (COC). NMR: 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3), (δ:ppm): 8.12 (d, J = 11.6 Hz, 2H, ArH), 7.7 (m, 4H, ArH), 7.34–7.4 (m, 2H, ArH), 7.08–7.25 (m, 4H, ArH), 6.82–0.7.1 (m, 8H, ArH), 4.5 (s, 8H, CH2 H), 3.72–3.65 (m, 16H, CH2 H), 3.37–3.48 (m, 16H, CH2 H), 3.3 (s, 12H, CH3 H), 2.25 (s, 12H, CH3 H), 1.97 (s, 12H, CH3 H)., (log ɛ): 682 (4.25), 618 (3.73), 357 (4.08).

To prepare the biotin functionalized GQDs, a solution of biotin (0.015 g, 0.06 mmol) in DMF (2.5 mL) was prepared. To this solution, DCC (0.0083 g, 0.05 mmol) and DMAP (0.01 g, 0.08 mmol), were added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. A separate solution of GQDs (0.1 g) in water (1 mL) was prepared and added to the biotin solution. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The GQDs-biotin were suspended in ethanol and then collected by centrifugation to remove non-conjugated biotin and GQDs, as well as DCC and DMAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of Pcs to GQDs/GQDs-biotin, Scheme 2
ce:section.ce:para: The Zn(II)Pcs were π-π stacked onto GQDs as previously described [18]. The GQDs (20 mg) and Pcs (4 and 5) (20 mg each) were separately dissolved in dry DMF and sonicated for 4 h. The mixtures were then stirred at room temperature in the dark for 48 h. The 4-GQDs were precipitated using ethanol, while 4-GQDs-biotin were precipitated using methanol. The 5-GQDs and 5-GQDs-biotin were precipitated out of solution using THF and washed with diethyl ether. The conjugates were collected by centrifugation and thereafter dried in a vacuum.

The synthesized phthalonitrile, Pc complexes, GQDs-biotin, Pcs-GQDs and Pcs-GQDs-biotin were structurally characterised using a series of analytical techniques including mass spectroscopy, proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), elemental analysis, Raman spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The results were in agreement with the expected results and hence, confirmed presence of target compounds.
The synthetic routes for 4 and 5 are illustrated in Scheme 1 , while the biotin-functionalized GQDs and the Pcs-GQDs π-π conjugation in Scheme 2 , using 4 and the GQDs-biotin as an example.
To evaluate the functional groups in the structures of the Pcs, GQDs and the conjugates, the FT-IR spectra were obtained, Fig. 1 .

The FT-IR spectrum of 3 shows the presence of the nitrile (CN) stretch at 2233 cm- [1], which is absent in the spectrum of the Zn(II)Pcs as seen with 4 (highlighted in red) Fig. 1. This is due to the utilization of the nitrile groups in the formation of Pcs, Scheme 1. The covalent linkage of biotin to GQDs was confirmed by the presence of the amide bond. The amide functional group in the GQDs-biotin was shown by the presence of the NH stretches at 3391 cm- 1 and 1646 cm- 1 , the C=O stretch at 1555 cm- 1 and CH stretch at 3295 cm- 1 which are characteristic to an amide group combined. A strong C=O stretch at 1700-1668 cm- 1 is seen in the biotin spectrum, this may be for both the carboxylic end and the carbonyl in the ring of the structure. The FT-IR spectrum if 4-GQDs-biotin does not show new bonds compared to 4 alone nor GQDs-biotin since no new bonds were formed.

Furthermore, EDX was used to determine the elemental composition of the GQDs in comparison to the GQDs-biotin, Fig. 2. The main elements present in both the structures include C at 0.2 keV, N at 0.4 keV and O at 0.6 keV, while GQDs-biotin affords S at 2.3 keV on the structure as shown in Fig. 2B, which is absent in the GQDs, Fig. 2A.

The TEM images of the pristine GQDs/GQDs-biotin and the conjugates were used to evaluate the shape, size distribution and an estimation of the particle sizes Fig. 3 A–C. The average sizes were further estimated using DLS (alongside the corresponding TEM images), Fig. 3.

The GQDs are circular in shape and are monodispersed as shown in Fig. 3A and B. However, the conjugates (using 5-GQDs-biotin as an example), Fig. 3C, appear aggregated compared to the pristine and biotin conjugated GQDs. This is likely to be due to interactions between the Pcs on adjacent GQDs via π-π stacking since Pcs are known for their π-π stacking to form H aggregates [25], and size estimation is therefore difficult to ascertain from the TEM images.

The DLS plots were obtained to determine the average particle sizes of the GQDs and GQDs-biotin complexes Fig. 3. The average diameter for GQDs obtained was 7.5 nm and approximately 12 nm for GQDs-biotin as shown in Fig. 3A and B. The average size for the 5-GQDs-biotin obtained was 24.2 nm (Table 1 ). An increase in the size of the GQDs-conjugates as compared to the pristine GQDs observed is due to the stacking of Pcs onto the surface of the GQDs. This is observed for all conjugates in Table 1.

The Pcs loading onto the surface of GQDs was determined using a quantitative method as described previously [26]. The Pc loadings on GQDs are summarised in Table 1. Although cationic Pcs attract to the surface of the GQDs by electrostatic attraction, the cations may reduce stacking of Pcs on each other through like-charge repulsion, hence the decrease in Pcs loading on GQDs upon introduction of cations. The Pcs loading further decreases when conjugated on GQDs-biotin. The presence of biotin on the edges of the GQDs may introduce steric hindrances, hence reduce Pcs loading compared to non-functionalized GQDs. However, the decrease in the loading of the Pcs is relatively low.

Raman spectroscopy was used to determine the degree of structural defects introduced on the surfaces of GQDs upon conjugation to biotin and Pc complexes Fig. 4 . The GQDs are known to display characteristic Raman peaks termed the G-Band (sp2 ) tangential mode and the D-(disorder) band (sp3) breathing mode. The G-band is a representation of in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, whereas the D band is due to the out of plane vibrations attributed to the presence of structural defects [27]. The D-bands are observed at 1200 cm−1 for GQDs, at 1240 cm−1 for GQDs-biotin and at 1271 cm−1 for 4-GQDs-biotin, whereas the G bands are observed at 1506 cm−1 for GQDs and 1537 cm−1 for GQDs-biotin and at 1579 cm−1 for the 4-GQDs-biotin, Fig. 4. Shifts in the Raman frequencies are often indicative of strong π-electron interactions in hybrid materials [28].

The ratio of the D and G intensities, denoted ID/IG, is generally considered as a quality parameter to determine the extent of functionalization of the carbon nanomaterials. The ID/IG obtained for GQDs is 0.10. The value increases upon the covalent conjugation to biotin in GQDs-biotin. This is due to the introduction of defects. The ID/IG however increases slightly upon π-π conjugation to 4, in 4-GQDs to 0.14 and drastically for 5-GQDs to 0.26, Table 1. For 4-GQDs this is expected since the ID/IG ratio is not expected to change drastically when the Pcs are π-π stacked. The more significant change for 5-GQDs are due to additional electrostatic interaction, more so for 5-GQDs-biotin, since biotin introduces out of plane functional groups and therefore increasing the D-Band.

The maximum absorbance observed for Pcs is denoted the Q-band, Fig. 5 A. This occurs at 684 nm for complex 4 and is slightly blue shifted to 682 nm for 5 in DMSO Table 1. The blue shift for 5 is due to the lowering of the electron donating ability of the nitrogen groups of 4 on quaternization [29]. In water, the Q-band is split for complex 5, with the low energy band being due to the monomer and the higher energy band near 650 nm (Table 1) being due to aggregation [25]. The solubility of 5 in water unlike complex 4 (which is not water soluble) was one way to monitor the introduction of cationic charges and formation of quaternary amines on the structure of complex 4. Lambert – Beer law was observed in DMSO for complexes 4 and 5, showing monomeric behaviour, Figs. S1 and S2 in the Supporting Information.
The UV-vis absorption spectra of the Pc 4 and its quaternized derivative, 5 are compared to their respective GQDs and GQDs-biotin conjugates, Fig. 6 A–C.

The Q bands for both complexes 4 and 5 in DMSO are slightly blue shifted in the presence of GQDs Fig. 6A and B, but there was no difference in the presence of biotin, Table 1. We have reported that the shifting of the Q band to the blue (or to red) in the presence of semiconductor QDs was dependant on the functionalities on the surface of the QDs [30]. The intensity of the B-band, between 300 nm and 400 nm increases upon conjugation for both 4 and 5. This is the sum of the absorbance intensities of the GQDs and Pcs and may be an indication of the presence of GQDs in the complexes, ideally π-π interaction. The GQDs absorb in the blue-region, with λ max at 365 nm in DMSO Fig. 6C. The absorbance slightly red-shifts when linked to biotin, with λ max at 367 nm in DMSO, Fig. 6C. The extra N groups introduced to the GQDs structure from the biotin may hold the same effect as does N groups in N-doped GQDs, which causes the absorption and emission of GQDs to red-shift [31].
The fluorescence parameters of the Pcs and their conjugates were determined. The UV-vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of complexes 4 and 5 are shown in Fig. 7 .

The absorption spectra of 4 and 5 are similar to their respective excitation spectra and mirror images to their emission spectra. This means that the same molecule that is emitting is the same as the one absorbing [32].

The fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) of 4 and 5 were determined by comparative methods as described in the literature [32], (equations are provided in the Supporting Information). Quinine sulfate in 0.05 M H2SO4 solution (ΦF = 0.52) [33] was used as the standard when determining the ΦF at excitation wavelength of GQDs, while the unsubstituted Zn(II)Pc (ΦF = 0.20) was used as standard for ΦF in DMSO [34] when exciting where Pcs absorb. For excitation where Pcs absorb, there are decreases in ΦF values for 4 and 5 upon conjugation, (4-GQDs, 4-GQDs-biotin and 5-GQDs, 5-GQDs-biotin, respectively), Table 2 . The decrease maybe attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer between the excited singlet state of the Pcs and the carbon material [35]. The ΦF of GQDs-biotin is higher compared to the pristine GQDs in both water and DMSO. In water, the ΦF value is higher for GQDs, but not too different for GQDs-biotin in the two solvents. When exciting where GQDs absorb, the ΦF of the GQDs in the Pcs-GQDs or Pc-GQDs-biotin conjugates decrease drastically, compared to the GQDs/GQDs-biotin alone when excited where GQDs absorb, 355 nm. This may be due to Fӧrster energy transfer (FRET) and other processes which quench the fluorescence of GQDs [36].

FRET is a phenomenon based on the transfer of energy from one emitting molecule (donor) to another absorbing molecule (acceptor) through dipole-dipole interaction between the two. FRET depends on the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor (GQDs) and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor (Pc). The spectral overlap between the GQDs and the Pcs is very week, Fig. 8 . However, the intensity of the emission spectrum of GQDs is reduced in the conjugates for both 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs when exciting where GQDs absorb, λ exc 355, Fig. 8. The decrease in intensity could due to non-FRET processes such as changes in surface defects [37]. Moreover, Pcs alone also absorb in the region where GQDs absorb, the B band. The emission spectra of the Pcs when excited at 355 nm (where GQDs absorb) are shown as inserts in Fig. 8A and B. Thus, the stimulated emission and FRET cannot certainly be confirmed.

Moreover, the decrease in the ΦF observed for the conjugates may additionally be due to other inter/intramolecular process, including photon induced electron transfer (PET), inter-charge transfer (ICT) involving physicochemical interactions between the substituents on the structure of the complexes [36].

The τF values of the complexes were determined using the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) and the decay curve is shown in Fig. 9 , using complex 4 as an example. The values are shown in Table 2. When excited where the Pcs absorb, the τF values became longer upon conjugation for the Zn(II)Pcs as observed with 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs, but shorter for 4-GQDs-biotin and 5-GQDs-biotin in DMSO. It is expected that the fluorescence lifetimes and yields should change in unison. However, increase or decrease could depend on the orientation of the fluorophore in the conjugate [39]. For excitation where GQDs absorb, the τF drastically decrease to negligible values (<0.01 ns, hence not added to Table 2), this may be due to the occurrence of processes described previously.

Triplet quantum yields (ΦT) and lifetimes (τT) were calculated using equations provided in the Supporting Information, where the ZnPc was used as the standard (ΦT = 0.65) in DMSO [40]. These values are used to determine the proportion of photons that undergo intersystem crossing to occupy the triplet excited state. The triplet decay curve is shown in Fig. 10 .

The ΦT of the Pcs increased upon conjugation for both 4 and 5, Table 2. For the conjugates with GQDs, increases in the ΦT values were accompanied by the shortening of the τT as expected [41]. Lengthening of triplet lifetimes of the Pcs is observed for GQDs-biotin, this could be due to protection of the Pcs by the GQDs-biotin.

The efficient interaction of the triplet state of the Zn(II)Pc photosensitisers with ground state (triplet) oxygen can result in the generation of singlet oxygen by energy transfer from the photo-activated sensitizer to the oxygen. To determine the singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ), the chemical photodegradation of the singlet oxygen quenchers (DPBF in DMSO and ADMA in water), were monitored using UV-vis spectroscopy, Fig. 11 . The ΦΔ values were determined using the equation previously described [42] and provided in the Supporting Information. ZnPc standard (ΦΔ = 0.67 in DMSO) [40] and AlPcSmix (containing a mixture of differently sulfonated phthalocyanines and synthesized as reported in the literature [43]) in aqueous media (ΦΔ = 0.42) [40] were used as standards.

The decrease in the absorbance of the DPBF in DMSO and ADMA in water, is indicative of photo-dependant singlet oxygen generation as shown in Fig. 11. The sustained Q-band of 5 during photoactivation suggests stability of the complex, this was the case for all samples.

The calculated values of ΦΔ values for the Zn(II)Pcs and their conjugates are summarised in Table 2. The ΦΔ decreases upon GQDs conjugation for complex 4 and its conjugates but increase for 5-GQDs and stay the same for 5-GQDs-biotin in DMSO. It is expected that ΦΔ would increase with the increase in the ΦT since the former is generated from the latter as is the case for 5 an its respective conjugates. For complex 4, however, the ΦΔ decreases upon conjugation to GQDs and GQDs-biotin. This decrease may be due the screening effect of the GQDs on the Pcs, which may shield the transfer of energy to oxygen, and hence the reduce ΦΔ [44]. This effect will be more severe for conjugates of complex 4 where the conjugates had larger DLS sizes compared to conjugates of 5, Table 1. The ΦΔ values for 5, 5-GQDs and 5-GQDs-biotin are reduced in water compared to DMSO, Table 2. This could be due to the reported quenching effect of water on the singlet oxygen generation of the phthalocyanine [34].

The photodynamic therapy activity of the Pcs and their respective conjugates was evaluated on the MCF-7 cell lines in vitro. The cells were grown to 100% confluency, with approximately 105 cell units, counted using a haemocytometer. The Pcs and conjugates were then inoculated at different concentrations ranging from 5 μg/mL – 100 μg/mL. The cells were incubated under growth conditions over 24 h to allow for photosensitizer uptake. The activity of the complexes was tested when incubated in the dark and under irradiation. Detailed information on in vitro studies are outlined in the Supporting Information.

The cells prior and post treatments were viewed under a microscope to visually asses the effects of the synergistic activity of light and the Pcs and conjugates. Fig. 12 A and B shows the effect under dark incubation and upon exposure to light in the presence of 5-GQDs-biotin respectively. There were no cytocidal effects observed for cells incubated in the dark for each of the studied complexes. In contrast, upon exposure to light, the number of viable cells reduced for all complexes/conjugates Fig. 12B. For control samples, 1% DMSO, pristine GQDs and GQDs-biotin, no cytotoxic effect was observed upon irradiation. For GQDs and GQDs biotin, no activity was expected since the light source was out of the range of absorbance for GQDs, hence no photo-activation to initiate cell death. For comparison purposes, 1% DMSO was employed for all samples since not all complexes are soluble in water without DMSO.

To quantify viable cells after irradiation, the cells were treated with WST and the absorbance at each test sample was obtained and calculated as a percentage form the total cells in the 1% DMSO control sample, as represented in the histograms, Fig. 13 .

For dark incubation, there is little or no decrease in the number of viable cells for each of the complexes, Fig. 13A. The cell viability decreased with an increase in concentration following irradiation, Fig. 13B.

The cytotoxic (PDT) activity of the complexes was higher (less cell viability) for non-conjugated 4 and 5 compared to their non-functionalized GQDs conjugates, Table 3 . For complex 4, the low PDT activity may be related to the low ΦΔ of the conjugates compared to 4 alone, Table 2. The conjugates of GQDs-biotin showed much more enhanced PDT activity compared to the conjugates of GQDs alone, Table 3. This may be a result of improved cellular uptake since cancer cells are known to overexpress biotin receptors. Moreover, the DMEM used in this study had no biotin content, and therefore, there was no binding competition between the biotin on the GQDs to nutrient biotin. The cationic complexes were generally better performing compared to the non-charged complexes. This has previously been reported for Pcs [45] and other charged complexes for cancers [46,47]. Where the net anionic membrane charge of cancer cells is believed to be susceptible to cationic drug complex through electrostatic attraction. This may result in improved drug retainment and hence more available Pcs for therapy. The combination of charge and biotin ligands improves the therapeutic efficacy of the complexes.

For PDT, the cells were incubated in the presence of Pcs, Pcs-GQDs or Pcs-GQDs-biotin at varying concentrations for 24 h prior irradiation. Therefore, the amounts of Pcs available for PDT after 24 h depends on the rate of uptake for each Pcs/conjugates.

The cells were grown to 100% confluency and inoculated with samples at concentrations similar to those used for cytotoxicity studies, and thereafter incubated over 24 h to allow for uptake into cells. The cells were then washed to remove floating Pcs and Pc conjugates. The fluorescence intensities were obtained for each sample, exciting at the λ max of Pcs for Pc s and Pcs-GQDs and λ max of GQDs for GQDs. A control sample containing cells with no Pcs or Pcs-GQDs/GQDs conjugates complexes was used.

The cellular uptake of the Pcs, GQDs and the conjugates increases upon increase in the concentration. There is an increase in the uptake upon introduction of cations in Pcs, and a further increase upon biotin conjugation observed for the Pcs-GQDs conjugates Fig. 14 A and B respectively. This may further justify the improvement of PDT in that improved cellular uptake avails more Pcs for PDT. The pristine GQDs and GQDs-biotin uptake continue to increase above 80 μg/mL Fig. 14C. The GQDs-biotin however, showed an increase in the concentration uptake when compared to GQDs alone. This increase may be afforded by the presence of biotin on the GQDs which enhance the uptake of the particles through a receptor mediated transport system.
The concentration intervals where the cellular uptake begins to plateau was determined, Table 4 .

5-GQDs-biotin show higher uptake compared to other complexes/conjugates, where the intensity increases up to the concentration interval of 60–80 μg/mL, Table 4. Although it may be assumed that there are more Pcs units in the non-conjugated Pcs samples than there are in their conjugates, the low uptake may be due to the absence of uptake aids such as receptor ligands and charge, more than complex saturation in the cells at higher concentrations, since 5-GQDs-biotin have proven to continue uptake even at higher concentrations. The uptake of GQDs and the GQDs-biotin continuously increased with increasing concentration. Therefore, the combination of charge on Pcs and biotin improves the PDT efficiency through the increase of Pc units available for therapy at a time.

The photophysical properties of complexes 4 and 5 and their corresponding GQDs and GQDs-biotin conjugates were determined including the ΦF, ΦT and ΦΔ. The ΦF of the complexes 4 and 5 decrease upon conjugation to GQDs. Consequently, the ΦT increases upon conjugation to GQDs, this is expected since ΦF and ΦT are competing processes. The ΦΔ for complex 4 (0.67) was however higher compared to complex 5 (0.53). A decrease in the ΦΔ of the complexes was observed in some cases upon conjugation to GQDs, this may be due to the screening effect. The conjugation of Pcs to non-functionalized GQDs did not show improvement in the in vitro PDT activity on MCF-7 cell lines. However, upon functionalization of GQDs with biotin, the PDT activity improves due to improved drug uptake in the Pcs-GQDs-biotin conjugates. In addition to assisted uptake using biotin, complex 5 and 5-conjugates showed better PDT activity, with the lowest cell viability of 34.9% after treatment. This is also believed to be due to the enhanced uptake of the cationic complexes. Overall, the combination of cancer drug-uptake methods, viz GQDs, cationic charges and biotin transport, help improve the therapeutic efficacy of Pcs during PDT in vitro by improving the concentration of Pcs penetrating the cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through the DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) and Rhodes University are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
DYPI_2019_163_Nene et al. 02-03-2019-supporting DYPI_2019_163_Nene et al. 02-03-2019-supporting

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.03.002.
